caveats: a: The operating mix varies by stone type and end use.
a: Addressability depends on sensor coverage, standardized controls, and equipment age.
rho: No representative quarry-specific autonomous completion study was located.
rho: The wide product mix makes workflow standardization uneven.
e: Published industry statistics do not disclose contract-processing share.
e: Some specialty stones may have different service attachment than construction aggregate.
s5: Gallup is cross-industry rather than mining-specific.
s5: The industry contains materially different stone types and asset profiles.
q: Competitive advantage is site- and end-use-specific rather than national.
q: Specialty stone may be more defensible than ordinary road aggregate.
d5: Aggregate-wide data are dominated by limestone and granite, which this NAICS excludes.
d5: Construction spending includes price effects and does not directly measure stone volume.
o: Individual functions can be outsourced without eliminating the firm-level operator requirement.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Revenue is principally per-ton stone sales and delivery charges to local or regional construction, infrastructure, landscaping, industrial, and specialty-material customers, with repeat purchase orders but only a small outsourced-service component from contract processing or managed material handling.
lens.excluded_activities: Dimension-stone operations, granite and ordinary limestone quarries, sand-and-gravel producers, captive sites serving only an integrated parent, greenfield mine development, and businesses without meaningful third-party recurring revenue.
lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market quarry and preparation-plant operators mining or beneficiating crushed stone other than limestone and granite, including traprock, sandstone, quartzite, marble, slate, volcanic material, and bituminous limestone or sandstone; genuine recurring contract or toll processing is included where present.

a.bridge: Census defines the industry around physical quarrying and stone beneficiation, while USGS describes construction aggregates as mined and processed materials. I map the minority of work in dispatch, quoting, planning, quality documentation, predictive-maintenance analysis, compliance, and customer administration to AI-addressable labor; the proxy likely overstates near-term addressability at small, lightly instrumented sites.
caveats: The operating mix varies by stone type and end use.
Addressability depends on sensor coverage, standardized controls, and equipment age.
a.change_evidence: Task-level labor-hour observations across the major 212319 stone types and plant configurations would materially change a.
a.rationale: AI can assist information-heavy workflows and plant diagnostics, but drilling, blasting, excavation, loading, hauling, crushing, screening, and mechanical repair remain physical and locally supervised.

d5.bridge: USGS reports total U.S. crushed-stone production at about 1.5 billion tons in both 2024 and 2025 and expects infrastructure plus manufacturing, energy, and data-center investment to support demand; Census May 2026 data show public highway-and-street construction up 2.9% year over year. I translate the stable broader category and positive end-market indicator to a modest five-year real demand multiplier for the non-limestone, non-granite subset; this may misstate specialty stone types whose end markets diverge from construction aggregate.
caveats: Aggregate-wide data are dominated by limestone and granite, which this NAICS excludes.
Construction spending includes price effects and does not directly measure stone volume.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year shipment and price forecasts by 212319 stone type and local haul market, linked to funded projects and capacity changes, would materially change d5.
d5.rationale: Infrastructure maintenance and construction support recurring consumption, but individual stone categories face local building cycles, weather, project timing, substitutes, and capacity additions.

caveats: Published industry statistics do not disclose contract-processing share.
Some specialty stones may have different service attachment than construction aggregate.
e.change_evidence: Firm-level revenue splits for product sales, delivery, contract processing, and managed services within the LMM population would materially change e.
e.rationale: The industry definition centers on extracting and preparing a physical product. Contract crushing, toll beneficiation, managed stockpiles, and closely bundled logistics can qualify, but ordinary repeat stone sales do not constitute a recurring outsourced service.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 212319 ($699,422,671) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 212319 ($2,923,794,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.2936 (margins.json[21]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Metals & Mining EBITDA/Sales 29.36%); revenue/employee $476,033 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 212319); class <5: 103 firms, avg revenue/firm $705,883 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $207,247 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 39 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,925,692 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $565,383 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 51 firms, avg revenue/firm $4,080,569 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,198,055 -> IN $1-10M band; class 20-99: 46 firms, avg revenue/firm $11,771,652 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,456,157 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 29 firms, avg revenue/firm $24,543,276 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $7,205,906 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 25 firms, avg revenue/firm $52,585,040 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $15,438,968 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 126

caveats: Individual functions can be outsourced without eliminating the firm-level operator requirement.
o.change_evidence: Evidence of mature quarry-as-a-service structures that transfer nearly all operating responsibility away from the asset owner would materially change o.
o.rationale: AI-enabled planning and controls do not remove the need for an operating entity to control reserves, permits, extraction, processing equipment, safety, environmental compliance, product quality, and physical fulfillment.

caveats: Competitive advantage is site- and end-use-specific rather than national.
Specialty stone may be more defensible than ordinary road aggregate.
q.change_evidence: Local-market delivered-price curves, permitted reserve lives, replacement-permit timelines, specification lock-in, customer concentration, and margin histories would materially change q.
q.rationale: Nearby permitted deposits, haul-distance economics, product specifications, customer qualification, and replacement lead times can protect incumbents. Defensibility is diluted by commodity pricing, substitute aggregates, public-bid exposure, and the heterogeneous availability of alternative stone sources.

caveats: No representative quarry-specific autonomous completion study was located.
The wide product mix makes workflow standardization uneven.
rho.change_evidence: Industry deployment data reporting autonomous completion, exception, override, and error rates for each addressable workflow would materially change rho.
rho.rationale: Standardized quoting, dispatch, reporting, document handling, and anomaly detection can be substantially automated, while safety-critical production decisions, geology, product-spec exceptions, maintenance authorization, and relationship management retain human review.

s5.bridge: Gallup reports that 22% of U.S. employer-business owners planned a sale or transfer within five years. I use this cross-industry intent rate as an anchor and discount the base for quarry transaction friction involving reserves, land or mineral rights, permits, equipment, reclamation, and environmental liabilities; the proxy could understate availability if this fragmented operator population is older than the employer-business average.
caveats: Gallup is cross-industry rather than mining-specific.
The industry contains materially different stone types and asset profiles.
s5.change_evidence: Completed five-year transaction counts against the eligible LMM 212319 firm population, together with owner-age and succession-plan data, would materially change s5.
s5.rationale: Owner succession should generate some opportunities, but stated intent converts imperfectly to completed quarry transactions and many assets will fail lens, reserve, permitting, or size screens.

principal_driver: Permitted deposits close to recurring customers can form durable local assets, with AI offering credible incremental productivity upside.
principal_weakness: The industry primarily sells mined material rather than outsourced services, and its residual-category heterogeneity reduces confidence in broad underwriting assumptions.

run_meta.title: Other Crushed and Broken Stone Mining and Quarrying

sources.supports: Aggregate is high-volume and low-value; new sources can be difficult to develop because of specifications, politics, transport cost, environmental concerns, land, processing needs, and distance to market.
